text: Bill Gates co-founded Microsoft with Paul Allen in 1975. He served as CEO until 2000, when Steve Ballmer took over. In 2014, Satya Nadella succeeded Ballmer and pivoted Microsoft toward cloud computing with Azure.

Steve Jobs co-founded Apple with Steve Wozniak in 1976. After being ousted in 1985, Jobs returned in 1997 and oversaw the launch of the iMac, iPod, iPhone, and iPad. Tim Cook succeeded Jobs as CEO of Apple in 2011.

Larry Page and Sergey Brin founded Google in 1998 while PhD students at Stanford. Eric Schmidt joined as CEO in 2001 and stepped down in 2011 when Page resumed the role. Sundar Pichai became CEO of Google in 2015.
Jeff Bezos founded Amazon in 1994 as an online bookstore. He stepped down as CEO in 2021, replaced by Andy Jassy. Bezos also founded Blue Origin, a private aerospace company, in 2000.

Mark Zuckerberg co-founded Facebook at Harvard in 2004 with Eduardo Saverin, Andrew McCollum, Dustin Moskovitz, and Chris Hughes. In 2021 the parent company was renamed Meta, signaling its pivot toward the metaverse.
Elon Musk co-founded PayPal (originally X.com, merged with Confinity) with Peter Thiel. After PayPal's sale to eBay in 2002, Musk founded SpaceX. He joined Tesla in 2004 and became CEO in 2008.

Sam Altman and Elon Musk co-founded OpenAI in 2015 along with Greg Brockman and Ilya Sutskever. Musk later departed the board. Altman became CEO of OpenAI and led the launches of GPT-3, ChatGPT, and GPT-4.

Linus Torvalds released the first Linux kernel in 1991 while a student at the University of Helsinki. Linux now powers most internet servers and underlies Android, which was acquired by Google in 2005.
Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN in 1989. He proposed HTML, HTTP, and URLs. Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn had earlier designed TCP/IP, the foundation of the internet itself.

Dennis Ritchie created the C programming language at Bell Labs in 1972 and co-authored Unix with Ken Thompson. Bjarne Stroustrup later designed C++ at the same lab, extending C with object-oriented features.

James Gosling led the development of Java at Sun Microsystems, released in 1995. Sun was acquired by Oracle in 2010. Gosling and Brian Kernighan are both associated with Bell Labs heritage in language design.
Donald Knuth, professor at Stanford, wrote The Art of Computer Programming and created TeX. He won the Turing Award in 1974 for fundamental contributions to algorithm analysis.
Geoffrey Hinton, Yoshua Bengio, and Yann LeCun shared the 2018 Turing Award for foundational work in deep learning. Hinton spent much of his career at the University of Toronto and Google.
Andrew Ng co-founded Coursera in 2012 with Daphne Koller. Previously at Stanford, Ng led the Google Brain project alongside Jeff Dean before joining Baidu as Chief Scientist.
Demis Hassabis co-founded DeepMind in 2010 with Shane Legg and Mustafa Suleyman. DeepMind was acquired by Google in 2014. The team built AlphaGo, which defeated Lee Sedol in 2016, and AlphaFold.
Travis Kalanick and Garrett Camp co-founded Uber in 2009 in San Francisco. After scandals in 2017, Dara Khosrowshahi replaced Kalanick as CEO. Khosrowshahi previously ran Expedia.
Brian Chesky, Joe Gebbia, and Nathan Blecharczyk founded Airbnb in 2008. The company went public on Nasdaq in December 2020. Chesky remains CEO.

Jack Dorsey co-founded Twitter in 2006 with Biz Stone, Evan Williams, and Noah Glass. Dorsey also founded Square (now Block) the same decade. Twitter was acquired by Elon Musk in 2022 and rebranded as X.
Reed Hastings and Marc Randolph founded Netflix in 1997. Hastings led the pivot from mail-order DVD rentals to streaming. Ted Sarandos became co-CEO with Hastings in 2020.
Patrick Collison and John Collison founded Stripe in 2010. The Irish brothers built it into a multi-billion dollar payments infrastructure company headquartered in San Francisco and Dublin.
Brian Acton and Jan Koum founded WhatsApp in 2009. Facebook acquired WhatsApp for 19 billion dollars in 2014. Both founders later left Meta over privacy disagreements.
Drew Houston and Arash Ferdowsi founded Dropbox at MIT in 2007. The company went public on Nasdaq in 2018. Houston serves as CEO.
Adi Shamir, Ron Rivest, and Leonard Adleman invented the RSA cryptosystem at MIT in 1977. The trio shared the Turing Award in 2002 for the algorithm that underpins modern public-key cryptography.

Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman published the Diffie-Hellman key exchange in 1976, pioneering public-key cryptography. They shared the Turing Award in 2015. Ralph Merkle contributed related work as a student.

Ada Lovelace wrote the first algorithm intended for a machine — Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine — in 1843. A century later Alan Turing proposed the universal computing machine in 1936 and helped break Enigma at Bletchley Park.

Marie Curie won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903 (shared with husband Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel) for radioactivity research. She won a second Nobel — in Chemistry — in 1911 for isolating radium and polonium.

Albert Einstein received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 for explaining the photoelectric effect. He had already published special relativity in 1905 and general relativity in 1915 while at the University of Bern and ETH Zurich.

Niels Bohr formulated his model of the atom in 1913 and won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1922. He founded the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics with Werner Heisenberg, who later won the Nobel in 1932.

Erwin Schrödinger and Paul Dirac shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics for the discovery of new productive forms of atomic theory. Schrödinger's wave equation and Dirac's relativistic equation both came in 1926-1928.

James Watson and Francis Crick proposed the DNA double-helix structure in 1953, working at the Cavendish Laboratory in Cambridge. They drew on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction Photograph 51. Watson, Crick, and Maurice Wilkins shared the 1962 Nobel Prize in Medicine.

Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin at St Mary's Hospital in London in 1928. Howard Florey and Ernst Chain later developed it into a usable antibiotic at Oxford. The three shared the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1945.

Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species in 1859, proposing evolution by natural selection. Alfred Russel Wallace independently developed the same theory and their papers were jointly read at the Linnean Society in 1858.

Gregor Mendel established the laws of inheritance through pea-plant experiments at the Abbey of St Thomas in Brno during the 1860s. His work was rediscovered around 1900 and became the foundation of classical genetics.

Dmitri Mendeleev published the periodic table of elements in 1869 while a professor at Saint Petersburg State University. He predicted the existence and properties of gallium, scandium, and germanium years before their discovery.

Stephen Hawking proposed that black holes emit thermal radiation in 1974 — now called Hawking radiation. He held the Lucasian Chair at Cambridge, the post once held by Isaac Newton. He never received the Nobel Prize.

Richard Feynman shared the 1965 Nobel Prize in Physics with Julian Schwinger and Sin-Itiro Tomonaga for quantum electrodynamics. Feynman also worked on the Manhattan Project at Los Alamos under J. Robert Oppenheimer.

Enrico Fermi led the team that produced the first controlled nuclear chain reaction at the University of Chicago in 1942. He had earlier won the 1938 Nobel Prize for neutron-induced radioactivity before fleeing fascist Italy to the U.S.

Jennifer Doudna and Emmanuelle Charpentier shared the 2020 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for developing CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing. Their 2012 paper transformed molecular biology, enabling precise edits to any genome.

Katalin Karikó and Drew Weissman shared the 2023 Nobel Prize in Medicine for discoveries enabling mRNA vaccines against COVID-19. Their work at the University of Pennsylvania underpinned both the Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna vaccines.
Vera Rubin's observations of galaxy rotation curves in the 1970s provided strong evidence for dark matter. She worked at the Carnegie Institution and inspired the Vera C. Rubin Observatory in Chile.

Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar received the 1983 Nobel Prize in Physics for his theory on the structure and evolution of stars. He worked at the University of Chicago for most of his career. The Chandrasekhar limit is named after him.

Roger Penrose, Reinhard Genzel, and Andrea Ghez shared the 2020 Nobel Prize in Physics for work on black holes. Genzel and Ghez separately demonstrated the supermassive black hole at the center of the Milky Way.

Linus Pauling won the 1954 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for understanding chemical bonds. He also won the 1962 Nobel Peace Prize for opposing nuclear weapons testing, making him one of only four people to win Nobel Prizes in different fields.
Tu Youyou received the 2015 Nobel Prize in Medicine for discovering artemisinin, an antimalarial derived from sweet wormwood. She was the first Chinese citizen to receive a science Nobel.

Barbara McClintock won the 1983 Nobel Prize in Medicine for discovering mobile genetic elements ('jumping genes') in maize. She worked at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory for most of her career, her work initially dismissed before being vindicated.

Mahatma Gandhi led India to independence from British rule in 1947 through nonviolent civil disobedience. He was assassinated in 1948 by Nathuram Godse. Jawaharlal Nehru, his political heir, became India's first Prime Minister.

Nelson Mandela led the African National Congress in opposition to apartheid in South Africa. After 27 years in prison, he was freed in 1990 and became the country's first Black president in 1994. He shared the 1993 Nobel Peace Prize with F.W. de Klerk.

Martin Luther King Jr. led the U.S. civil rights movement and won the 1964 Nobel Peace Prize at age 35. He was assassinated in Memphis in 1968. His 'I Have a Dream' speech was delivered at the 1963 March on Washington.

Mikhail Gorbachev introduced perestroika and glasnost as General Secretary of the Soviet Union starting 1985. He won the 1990 Nobel Peace Prize and oversaw the dissolution of the USSR by 1991, succeeded by Boris Yeltsin in Russia.

Margaret Thatcher served as British Prime Minister from 1979 to 1990, the first woman in the role. She was succeeded by John Major. Thatcher's policies of privatization and deregulation defined modern Conservative Party politics.
Angela Merkel served as Chancellor of Germany from 2005 to 2021, the first woman in the post. A trained physicist from East Germany, she led the CDU and was succeeded by Olaf Scholz of the SPD.

Barack Obama, the first Black U.S. president, served two terms from 2009 to 2017. He won the 2009 Nobel Peace Prize early in his presidency. Joe Biden served as his vice president and later succeeded Donald Trump as president in 2021.

Winston Churchill served as British Prime Minister during World War II, succeeding Neville Chamberlain in 1940. He led the wartime coalition with Franklin Roosevelt and Joseph Stalin, and was awarded the 1953 Nobel Prize in Literature.

Franklin D. Roosevelt served four terms as U.S. president from 1933 to 1945, leading the country through the Great Depression and most of World War II. He was succeeded by Harry Truman, who authorized the atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki.

Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 12, 1961, aboard Vostok 1. Eight years later Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first to walk on the Moon during Apollo 11, while Michael Collins orbited above.

Genghis Khan founded the Mongol Empire in 1206, eventually the largest contiguous land empire in history. His grandson Kublai Khan completed the conquest of China and founded the Yuan dynasty in 1271.

Julius Caesar crossed the Rubicon in 49 BCE, triggering civil war against Pompey. After becoming dictator he was assassinated in 44 BCE by senators including Brutus. His grandnephew Octavian, later Augustus, became the first Roman emperor.

Cleopatra VII was the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt, allied first with Julius Caesar and later with Mark Antony. After their defeat at Actium by Octavian in 31 BCE, both Antony and Cleopatra took their own lives.

Queen Elizabeth II reigned as monarch of the United Kingdom from 1952 to 2022, the longest reign in British history. She was succeeded by her son Charles III. Her reign spanned fifteen Prime Ministers from Winston Churchill to Liz Truss.

Volodymyr Zelenskyy was elected President of Ukraine in 2019. After Russia's full-scale invasion in February 2022, his wartime leadership made him a globally recognized figure. He had previously been a television actor.

William Shakespeare wrote Hamlet, Macbeth, Romeo and Juliet, and King Lear in Elizabethan and Jacobean London. He co-owned the Globe Theatre and worked alongside Christopher Marlowe and Ben Jonson in the period of English literature now bearing his name.

Leonardo da Vinci painted the Mona Lisa and The Last Supper during the Italian Renaissance. He worked in Milan, Florence, and Rome and was patronized by Ludovico Sforza and later King Francis I of France, in whose arms he reportedly died in 1519.

Vincent van Gogh painted The Starry Night, Sunflowers, and Self-Portrait with Bandaged Ear in his final years. His brother Theo van Gogh, an art dealer, supported him throughout. Vincent died in 1890 in Auvers-sur-Oise.

Pablo Picasso, alongside Georges Braque, founded Cubism in Paris around 1907. Picasso's Guernica (1937) responded to the Spanish Civil War bombing. His Blue Period and Rose Period preceded the Cubist breakthrough.

Salvador Dalí painted The Persistence of Memory (1931), the melting-clock surrealist icon. He was associated with André Breton's Surrealist group in Paris before being expelled. His later years were spent at the Theatre-Museum in Figueres, Spain.

Edvard Munch painted The Scream in Oslo in 1893, capturing modern existential anxiety. Norway later commemorated him on its 1000-krone banknote. Four versions of the painting exist in different media.

Johann Sebastian Bach composed the Brandenburg Concertos, the Goldberg Variations, and over 1000 catalogued works. He worked in Leipzig as Cantor at St Thomas Church from 1723 until his death in 1750.

Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, child prodigy from Salzburg, composed The Magic Flute, Don Giovanni, and the Requiem before dying in Vienna in 1791 at age 35. His rivalry with Antonio Salieri is dramatized — and exaggerated — in the film Amadeus.
Ludwig van Beethoven composed nine symphonies including the Ninth ('Choral'), continuing to write even after losing his hearing. He worked in Vienna and was mentored briefly by Joseph Haydn.

The Beatles — John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, and Ringo Starr — formed in Liverpool in 1960 and disbanded in 1970. Lennon was murdered outside the Dakota in New York in 1980 by Mark David Chapman.
The Rolling Stones formed in London in 1962. Mick Jagger and Keith Richards remain the songwriting core. Founding member Brian Jones drowned in 1969; Charlie Watts, longtime drummer, died in 2021.
Steven Spielberg directed Jaws, E.T., Schindler's List, and Jurassic Park, winning Best Director Oscars in 1994 and 1999. He co-founded DreamWorks with Jeffrey Katzenberg and David Geffen in 1994.

James Cameron directed Titanic (1997) and Avatar (2009), the first films to gross over one and two billion dollars respectively. Cameron has dived to the Mariana Trench in the Deepsea Challenger submersible he designed.

Christopher Nolan directed Memento, Inception, Interstellar, and Dunkirk before winning his first Best Director Oscar for Oppenheimer in 2024. Oppenheimer dramatizes Robert Oppenheimer's leadership of the Manhattan Project.
Bong Joon-ho directed Parasite, which won the 2020 Best Picture Oscar — the first non-English-language film to do so. He had previously directed Snowpiercer and The Host.

Michael Jordan won six NBA Championships with the Chicago Bulls in the 1990s, alongside Scottie Pippen and coach Phil Jackson. Jordan won five MVPs and is widely regarded as the greatest basketball player ever.
Kobe Bryant won five NBA Championships with the Los Angeles Lakers, three alongside Shaquille O'Neal under Phil Jackson, and two more with Pau Gasol. He died in a helicopter crash in 2020.

LeBron James won NBA championships with the Miami Heat (2012, 2013), Cleveland Cavaliers (2016), and Los Angeles Lakers (2020). He became the NBA's all-time leading scorer in 2023, passing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar.
Roger Federer won 20 Grand Slam singles titles. His longtime rivals Rafael Nadal (22 majors) and Novak Djokovic (24 majors) form the Big Three of modern men's tennis.
Serena Williams won 23 Grand Slam singles titles, the most in the Open Era. Her sister Venus Williams won seven majors. The pair often faced each other in Grand Slam finals.
Usain Bolt set the 100-meter world record of 9.58 seconds at the 2009 World Championships in Berlin. The Jamaican sprinter won eight Olympic gold medals across the 2008, 2012, and 2016 Games.

Lionel Messi led Argentina to the 2022 FIFA World Cup victory in Qatar. Previously at FC Barcelona, he won seven Ballon d'Or awards. His longtime rival Cristiano Ronaldo has won five Ballon d'Or awards.

Tom Brady won seven Super Bowl championships, six with the New England Patriots under coach Bill Belichick and a seventh with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers in 2021. He retired in 2023 as the NFL's all-time passing yards leader.

Tiger Woods has won 15 major championships in golf, second only to Jack Nicklaus's 18. Woods broke through at the 1997 Masters at age 21 and returned from a near-career-ending back injury to win the 2019 Masters.

Simone Biles is the most decorated gymnast in World Championship history. She withdrew from several events at the Tokyo 2020 Olympics citing mental health, then returned to win team gold at Paris 2024.

Plato founded the Academy in Athens around 387 BCE and taught Aristotle, who later founded the Lyceum. Plato was himself the student of Socrates, completing a famous teacher-student chain across three generations of Greek philosophy.

Aristotle tutored Alexander the Great at the Macedonian court before returning to Athens. After Plato's Academy passed to others, Aristotle established his own school, the Lyceum, where he wrote on logic, ethics, and natural science.

Confucius (Kong Fuzi) developed a system of ethics and governance in the Spring and Autumn period of ancient China. His teachings — collected by disciples in the Analects — shaped East Asian thought for two millennia.

Siddhartha Gautama, the Buddha, founded Buddhism in the Indian subcontinent around the 5th century BCE after attaining enlightenment under the Bodhi tree at Bodh Gaya. His core teachings became the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path.
Karl Marx co-authored The Communist Manifesto with Friedrich Engels in 1848 and later wrote Das Kapital. Marx lived in exile in London for the last half of his life, supported financially by Engels.

Warren Buffett took control of Berkshire Hathaway in 1965 and built it into a holding company spanning insurance, railroads, and consumer goods. His longtime partner Charlie Munger, who died in 2023, served as vice chairman.

Sam Walton founded Walmart in 1962 in Rogers, Arkansas. By the time he died in 1992 Walmart was the largest retailer in the United States. His family remained majority owners; son Rob Walton later served as chairman.
Ingvar Kamprad founded IKEA in Sweden in 1943 at age 17. The flat-pack furniture company grew to become the world's largest furniture retailer; Kamprad remained involved until his death in 2018.

Henry Ford founded the Ford Motor Company in 1903 and pioneered the moving assembly line for the Model T. The technique cut production time dramatically and made the automobile affordable to the middle class.
Coco Chanel founded the House of Chanel in Paris in 1910. She introduced the little black dress and Chanel No. 5 perfume, transforming women's fashion in the early 20th century.

Walt Disney co-founded the Disney Brothers Cartoon Studio with brother Roy in 1923. He created Mickey Mouse in 1928 and opened Disneyland in 1955. Disney died in 1966, three years before EPCOT was conceived.

Soichiro Honda founded Honda Motor Company in 1948 in Hamamatsu, Japan. The company grew from motorcycles into a global automaker. Honda himself remained a hands-on engineer-CEO well into his seventies.
Akio Morita co-founded Sony with Masaru Ibuka in 1946. Sony produced the world's first commercial transistor radio in 1955 and the Walkman in 1979, defining consumer electronics for decades.

Howard Schultz transformed Starbucks from a Seattle bean roaster into a global coffee chain after joining in 1982 and acquiring it in 1987. He served as CEO across three separate stints and retired in 2023, replaced by Laxman Narasimhan.

Peter Drucker, born in Vienna and later teaching at Claremont Graduate University, founded modern management as a discipline. His 1954 book The Practice of Management defined the role of the manager for the 20th century corporation.
